name: Temporal Reasoning
description: Tests temporal sequencing with events planted out of chronological order.

facts.content: Got promoted to mid-level developer
facts.planted_as: Two months ago I got promoted to mid-level developer.

facts.content: Started learning Python on my own
facts.planted_as: About a year and a half ago, I started learning Python on my own.

facts.content: Completed an advanced React course
facts.planted_as: Four months ago I completed an advanced React course.

facts.content: Got my first junior developer job at a startup
facts.planted_as: A year ago I got my first junior developer job at a startup.

facts.content: Switched to a larger company and learned C#
facts.planted_as: Eight months ago I switched to a larger company and learned C# there.

facts.content: Led my first project as tech lead
facts.planted_as: Last month I led my first project as tech lead!

noise_between_facts: The coffee machine at work broke again today.
I need to update my LinkedIn profile.
My teammate recommended a great coding podcast.

queries.question: Can you put my career milestones in chronological order, from earliest to most recent?
What was the first thing I learned in my tech career?
What's the most recent career milestone I mentioned?

distractor_turns.user: My friend just got their AWS cert too — said the exam was brutal, took her 3 attempts
distractor_turns.assistant: Three attempts shows real persistence! AWS certs are notoriously difficult.

distractor_turns.user: The company announced a hackathon for Q2, same format as last year
distractor_turns.assistant: Exciting! Are you planning to participate again this year?

distractor_turns.user: A recruiter from Google reached out to me — third time this month
distractor_turns.assistant: They're persistent! Are you considering it?

distractor_turns.user: My colleague just finished a coding bootcamp — she's switching from marketing to dev
distractor_turns.assistant: That's a big career change! How's she finding the transition?

distractor_turns.user: My team lead started mentoring interns last month, she's great at it
distractor_turns.assistant: Mentoring is so rewarding. I'm sure the interns appreciate her guidance.

distractor_turns.user: I heard the QA team is hiring — they need someone with telecom experience
distractor_turns.assistant: Telecom QA can be complex. Sounds like a specialized role.

noise_between_facts: The coffee machine at work broke again today.
I need to update my LinkedIn profile.
My teammate recommended a great coding podcast.
I've been thinking about taking a sabbatical next year.
My team just switched from Slack to Teams messaging.
We're planning a team building retreat next month.
The office is getting renovated — might be annoying.
I've been considering switching from VS Code to JetBrains.
Someone mentioned a new framework that's trending.

facts.content: Worked as a QA engineer for six months before transitioning to dev
facts.planted_as: So before I got into actual development, I spent about six months in QA. Honestly, it was eye-opening — breaking other people's code taught me more about edge cases than any tutorial ever did.

facts.content: Attended a coding bootcamp 18 months ago to switch careers
facts.planted_as: Actually, let me backtrack — the whole tech thing started when I enrolled in this intensive coding bootcamp about 18 months ago. I was working a completely different job and just decided to make the leap.

facts.content: Got AWS certified about 5 months ago just before switching companies
facts.planted_as: I also got my AWS certification about 5 months ago, just before I switched companies.

facts.content: Before QA, worked 2 years in customer support at a telecom
facts.planted_as: Way before all the tech stuff, I worked in customer support at a telecom company for about 2 years. That's where I first realized I wanted to build things, not just troubleshoot them.

facts.content: Completed a side project mobile app about 9 months ago
facts.planted_as: Around 9 months ago, I finished a side project — a mobile app for tracking workouts. Took me like three months of evenings and weekends but it felt amazing to ship something on my own.

facts.content: Started mentoring junior devs about 6 weeks ago
facts.planted_as: About 6 weeks ago I started mentoring junior developers on the team — really enjoy it.

facts.content: Got rejected from Google interviews about 7 months ago
facts.planted_as: I actually applied to Google about 7 months ago but didn't make it past the technical round. It stung at the time but honestly it motivated me to study harder.

facts.content: Won an internal hackathon about 3 months ago with a CI/CD automation tool
facts.planted_as: About 3 months ago I won our company's internal hackathon — built a CI/CD automation tool that saved the team like 4 hours a week.

facts.content: Started attending tech meetups 14 months ago to network
facts.planted_as: I started going to tech meetups around 14 months ago — great for networking.

facts.content: Got an offer from a fintech startup 3 weeks ago but turned it down
facts.planted_as: Actually, just 3 weeks ago I got an offer from a fintech startup, but I turned it down — too early to leave.

facts.content: Completed a Docker certification about 11 months ago right after joining the startup
facts.planted_as: I also did a Docker certification about 11 months ago, right after I started at the startup.

queries.question: Walk me through your entire career progression from the very beginning — customer support, QA, bootcamp, first dev job, and everything after. Put it in chronological order.
What was your first formal training that led to your tech career?
What certifications have you earned, and in what order did you get them?
Between the Google rejection and your recent fintech offer, what milestones happened in between?
How long were you at your first dev job before you switched companies?
What's the most recent thing that happened in your career and what's the oldest event you mentioned?
What were you doing around 9-11 months ago? List everything from that period.
Did you get your AWS certification before or after your Google rejection?
Did you start mentoring junior devs before or after winning the hackathon?
Which came first — completing your side project mobile app or getting the Docker certification?
Did you start attending tech meetups before or after your QA engineer role?

facts.content: Gave a lightning talk at a local JS meetup about 4 months ago
facts.planted_as: I actually gave my first lightning talk at a local JavaScript meetup about 4 months ago. I was terrified but the feedback was surprisingly positive.

facts.content: Started contributing to an open source project 2 months ago
facts.planted_as: About 2 months ago I started contributing to an open source Kubernetes tool — my first real OSS contribution. Already got two PRs merged.

facts.content: Passed the system design interview at Amazon but declined the offer last month
facts.planted_as: Remember when I failed the Google interview? Well, last month I actually passed the system design round at Amazon. They made an offer but I declined — decided I want to stay and grow where I am.
facts.assistant_response: That's a big deal — passing Amazon's system design round shows real growth since the Google experience. And it sounds like you made a thoughtful decision to stay. I'll remember that.

facts.content: Enrolled in a part-time master's program in distributed systems starting this fall
facts.planted_as: Big news — I just got accepted into a part-time master's program in distributed systems. Starts this fall. It'll be intense juggling work and school but I'm excited.
facts.assistant_response: Congratulations on getting accepted! A master's in distributed systems will complement your hands-on experience perfectly. I'll keep that in mind as we discuss your schedule going forward.

queries.question: What happened between your Google rejection and your Amazon interview? Walk me through each milestone with approximate dates.
What are your plans for professional development in the coming year?
